macro_segments.title: Introduction to Daf 29 and Overview of Third Perek

micro_segments.title: Context of Daf 29 as Final Daf of Third Perek and Discussion of Korban for Oath Violations
Foundation of Shevuot Mechanics: Shevua vs. Neder, Shevua Chala Al Shevua, Mushba Ve'omed
Transition to Next Perek: Use of Oaths in Court Deliberations and Litigation

macro_segments.title: Definition and Categories of Shevuat Shav (Oath Taken in Vain)

micro_segments.title: Distinction Between Shevuat Bituy, Shevuat Sheker, and Shevuat Shav with Examples
First Category of Shevuat Shav: Swearing About Something Known to Be Different (Stone Pillar as Gold)
Second Category: Swearing About Impossible or Patently False Claims (Man as Woman, Camel Flying)
Third Category: Swearing to Negate a Mitzvah Obligation (Not Building Sukkah, Not Taking Lulav)
Penalties for Shevuat Shav: Lashes for Intent, Exemption for Negligence, No Korban Required

macro_segments.title: The Principle of Obviousness and the Role of Three Witnesses in Determining Shevuat Shav

micro_segments.title: Ulla's Teaching: Shevuat Shav Requires Knowledge by Three People, Not Necessarily Everyone
Challenge to the Three-Person Standard: Lying Effectively Despite Known Facts
Discussion of What Constitutes 'Known' and Contextual vs. Universal Knowledge

macro_segments.title: Analysis of Impossible Oaths: Flying Camel and the Tossafot Interpretation

micro_segments.title: Gemara's Problem: What is the Actual Oath Content in 'If I Did Not See a Flying Camel'
Abuye vs. Rava: Different Girsa (Text Version) and Interpretation of the Flying Camel Case
Tossafot's Resolution: The Real Issue is Oath to Violate Practical Impossibility (Never Eating)
Discussion of Whether Neder on All Food Takes Effect and Concept of Neder Shav

macro_segments.title: The Centrality of God's Name to Shevua and Distinction from Neder

micro_segments.title: Core Function of Shevua: Invocation of God as Witness to Truth of Statement
Difference Between Shevuat Shav (Invokes God's Name) and Neder Shav (Does Not Exist as Concept)

macro_segments.title: The Role of Intent and Private Meaning in Shevua: The Trick of the Stick

micro_segments.title: Ravina's Question: Can Personal Meaning Change Truth of Oath (Pet Bird Named Camel)
Principle: Go by Lips Not Mind—Standard Meanings Cannot Be Overridden by Private Intent
Baraita on Court-Imposed Oaths: Warning That Oath Binds by Court's Understanding, Not Taker's
Rava's Trick: Giving Michael a Hollow Stick to Satisfy 'Giving $100' Without Intent
Resolution: Fear is Context (Not Private Meaning)—Oath Valid if Words Mean What Normally Understood

macro_segments.title: Moshe's Oath at Arvot Moav: God's Intent vs. Private Meanings

micro_segments.title: Source in Torah: Moshe Requires Bnei Yisrael to Take Oath on God's Terms, Not Their Own
Tossafot's Question: Why Do Oaths Enhance Obligation if Mitzvot Already Bind?
Pachad Yitzchak's Insight: Independent Ethics of Self-Obligation Beyond Divine Command
Closing Reason for 'On God's Da'at': Prevents Attempt to Nullify Oath Without Consideration of Divine Will

macro_segments.title: Practical Application: Communal Cherem and Waiver of Court-Imposed Oaths

micro_segments.title: Tossafot on Cherem Rabbeinu Gershom: If on Da'at Rabbim, How Can It Be Waived?
Resolution: Da'at Rabbim Prevents Individual from Self-Help but Gives Rabbis Full Discretion to Waive
Why Mention God's Da'at in Communal Cherem: Geonim's Stringency, Not Essential to Legal Mechanism

macro_segments.title: The Case of Contradictory Oaths: Eating and Not Eating the Same Bread

micro_segments.title: Mishnah: Swearing to Eat and Not Eat Same Bread—Which Oath is Binding?
Gemara's Problem: Second Oath is Shevuat Shav—When Does It Become Shav?
Rabbi Yirmiyah's Resolution: Both Oaths Stand; Violating Either Violates Both Simultaneously

macro_segments.title: The Centrality of God's Name to Shevua and Distinction from Neder (continued)

micro_segments.title: Rambam's Four Categories of Shevuat Shav and Role of Explicit God's Name

macro_segments.title: Shevuat Bituy: Characteristics and Scope of Future-Oriented Binding Oath

micro_segments.title: Scope of Shevuat Bituy: Applies to Men and Women, Relatives and Non-Relatives, Kosher and Disqualified
Jurisdiction Independence: Applies in Court and Outside Court, Self-Initiated Only

macro_segments.title: Shevuat Bituy: Characteristics and Scope of Future-Oriented Binding Oath (continued)

micro_segments.title: Penalties: Lashes for Willful Violation, Korban for Unintentional

macro_segments.title: Shevuat Shav Scope and Penalties: Broader Application Than Shevuat Bituy

micro_segments.title: Scope: Applies to Men, Women, Relatives, Non-Relatives, Qualified and Disqualified Witnesses
Jurisdiction: Works In Court and Outside, But Self-Initiated Only
Penalties: Lashes for Willful, Exemption for Unintentional (No Korban)

macro_segments.title: Oath Imposed by Others vs. Self-Imposed: When Does Saying Amen Count as Self-Imposed

micro_segments.title: Mishnah's Apparent Contradiction: Initially States Must Be Self-Imposed, Ends Saying Imposed Ones Are Binding
Shmuel's Resolution: Saying Amen After Imposed Oath Equals Self-Imposed (Amen Unpacks to Full Oath)
Rav Pappa and Ravina: Confirmation from Shevuat Ha'edut Rules About Court-Imposed Oaths
Reconciliation: Imposed Oath Binding Only When Amen Spoken; Otherwise Requires Court Context

macro_segments.title: Close Reading of Mishnah: Diuk BeMattanit and Implied Levels of Obligation

micro_segments.title: Shmuel's Teaching: How to Extract Laws from Precise Language of Mishnah
Rambam's Application: Listening to Bracha Creates Different Level Than Answering Amen
